verifications.evidence: Employer-side FICA alone is 7.65%, before FUTA/SUTA, workers comp, PEO/EOR admin, malpractice, credentialing, recruiting, support, and no-show/refund leakage. A 15% fee on nurse wages is likely insufficient unless the fee is charged on top of fully loaded labor cost.
verifications.note: The claim needs the fee base defined: wage, gross payroll, or fully loaded bill rate.

verifications.evidence: The argument proves hospitals have existing options and procurement friction, but it does not prove those options solve last-minute RN gaps well enough in the launch metro. VMS/MSP existence challenges go-to-market, not the underlying unmet staffing pain.
verifications.note: Launch-metro fill-rate and call-out data would decide this.

verifications.evidence: Last-minute nurse supply is price-sensitive and already served by agencies/gig apps that can pay high rates; W-2 onboarding and payroll burden reduce flexibility and margin. Without demonstrated shift density and accepted nurse pay rates, the supply-side economics are doubtful.
verifications.note: The claim needs real nurse acquisition and accepted-rate data.

verifications.evidence: Document verification is not the same as hospital clearance. Facility-specific competencies, immunizations, background/drug checks, EMR access, unit orientation, specialty validation, and audit requirements can block instant deployment even if an API returns fast records.
verifications.note: API speed does not equal floor-level acceptance.

verifications.evidence: The argument shows competition and weak moat, but the claim is about meaningful differentiation, not defensibility forever. If localized operations produce faster reliable fill in a metro, that can be differentiated even if incumbents could copy it.
verifications.note: The claim still needs measured fill-time advantage versus incumbents.

verifications.evidence: Clinical staffing risk can be insured in principle, but last-minute placement into unfamiliar units raises patient-safety, indemnity, deductible, exclusion, and defense-cost risk. On a $500k capital base, one serious incident or expensive policy terms could plausibly break the model.
verifications.note: This is less fatal if the launch excludes high-acuity units and insurance terms are proven.

verifications.evidence: Hospital procurement, legal, vendor credentialing, and security review can take months, and founder access usually cannot bypass compliance. But $500k sufficiency depends on burn rate, existing relationships, pilot scope, and whether demand is pre-committed.
verifications.note: The argument is plausible but too generic to defeat the claim by itself.

verifications.evidence: VMS/MSP channels are common for contingent staffing, but the argument overstates by saying hospitals route all staffing exclusively through them. Some facilities use direct agency or app workflows, and a standalone app could be approved or integrated, though fees would pressure margin.
verifications.note: The objection weakens the model but does not prove non-adoption.

verifications.evidence: Ultra-fast guaranteed fill likely requires excess available nurses, on-call incentives, or high premiums. However, intermittent W-2 nurses do not automatically create large fixed wage costs, so the specific fixed-cost/compliance objection is overstated while the liquidity guarantee remains unproven.
verifications.note: The real test is availability economics, not W-2 status alone.
